relationship_to_restored_text: Interpretive contemplative layer aligned by verse. This does not replace the source-audited restored translation.

verses.mystical_translation: Jesus crosses the Galilean sea.
A great crowd follows because signs of healing have been seen.
He sits on the mountain with disciples.
Passover nearness frames the bread sign.
Jesus lifts his eyes to the crowd and asks Philip about bread.
The question tests, while Jesus already knows the gift to come.
Philip calculates scarcity.
Andrew enters the scene.
A little boy’s loaves and fish seem too small.
Jesus makes space for the crowd on much grass.
Thanks turns small bread and fish into abundance.
Broken pieces are gathered so nothing is lost.
Twelve baskets hold the abundance beyond eating.
The sign makes the people name the Prophet.
Jesus refuses seizure into crowd-made kingship.
Evening lowers the disciples to the sea.
Darkness comes before Jesus arrives to them.
Wind stirs the sea.
Fear rises as Jesus walks near over the waters.
I am; do not fear.
Receiving him brings the boat to arrival.
The crowd tries to account for the crossing.
Other boats come near the place of thankful bread.
The crowd seeks Jesus across the sea.
They ask when he came.
Jesus reveals their seeking is belly-full more than sign-seeing.
Work for food remaining into age-long life.
They ask what divine work requires.
The work is trust in the Sent One.
They ask for another sign to support trust.
They remember manna as bread from heaven.
The Father gives the true bread from heaven.
Divine bread comes down and gives life to the world.
They ask always for this bread.
Jesus reveals: I am the bread of life.
Seeing has not yet become trust.
The coming one will not be cast out.
He comes from heaven to do the Sender’s will.
The Sender’s will is that nothing given be lost.
Beholding and trusting the Son opens life and last-day raising.
The bread-from-heaven claim provokes grumbling.
Known family origins become a barrier to heavenly origin.
Jesus tells them to stop grumbling.
The Father draws the one who comes.
The prophets promise divine teaching.
The one from the Divine has seen the Father.
Trust has age-long life.
I am the bread of life.
Manna fed the ancestors, yet they died.
This bread comes from heaven so one may not die.
The living bread gives life to the world.
Literal dispute rises around eating his embodied life.
Participation in the Son of Humanity’s life is necessary for life within.
Eating and drinking imagery points to deep participation and raising.
His life is true food and true drink.
Participation becomes mutual remaining: in me and I in that one.
Life flows from the living Father through the Sent One to the participant.
This bread differs from manna because it opens life into the age.
He teaches these things in Capernaum’s synagogue.
Many disciples call the word hard to hear.
Jesus knows their grumbling and asks if the saying causes stumbling.
The ascending Son of Humanity will deepen the mystery.
Spirit makes alive; his words are Spirit and life.
Jesus knows mistrust and the coming handing-over.
Coming to him is gift from the Father.
Many turn back and stop walking with him.
Jesus asks the Twelve whether they also want to leave.
Peter answers: to whom shall we go? You have words of age-long life.
Trust and knowing confess the Holy One of the Divine.
Even among the chosen Twelve, adversarial movement is present.
Judas is named as the one about to hand him over.
